news_summary: Today's space news highlights exciting advancements in the search for extraterrestrial intelligence sparked by a rare interstellar visitor and groundbreaking discoveries about Earth's ancient impact history.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées passionnantes dans la recherche d'une intelligence extraterrestre, suscitées par la visite d'un rare visiteur interstellaire, ainsi que des découvertes révolutionnaires sur l'histoire des impacts subis par la Terre dans le passé.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances emocionantes en la búsqueda de inteligencia extraterrestre, impulsados por un visitante interestelar excepcional, así como descubrimientos revolucionarios sobre la historia de los antiguos impactos en la Tierra.

articles.title: A rare interstellar visitor triggered a SETI search for alien technology
articles.summary: SETI scientists searched the interstellar object 3I/ATLAS for radio signals that could indicate extraterrestrial technology but found nothing beyond human-made interference. Even so, the rapid-response observations helped confirm the object's natural origin and showcased how future interstellar visitors can be investigated for signs of intelligent life.
articles.content:
Scientists with the SETI Institute have completed a search for possible technological signals coming from 3I/ATLAS, the third known interstellar object to pass through our Solar System. Using the Allen Telescope Array (ATA) at Northern California's Hat Creek Radio Observatory, researchers examined a broad range of radio frequencies for evidence of extraterrestrial technology. As expected based on previous observations, they found no signs of artificial signals, further supporting the conclusion that 3I/ATLAS is a natural comet-like object.

First identified in July 2025, 3I/ATLAS is only the third confirmed interstellar object ever observed entering our Solar System. It follows the discoveries of 1I/'Oumuamua and 2I/Borisov, both of which originated outside our planetary neighborhood.

Because it formed around another star, 3I/ATLAS offers scientists a valuable chance to study material from a different stellar system and gain insights into how planets and planetary systems develop. Although current evidence strongly points to a natural origin, researchers also view interstellar visitors as important targets in the search for technosignatures. In the unlikely event that one of these objects were artificial, it could reveal detectable technology and potentially provide the first evidence of life beyond Earth.

"Eventually, our own Voyager spacecraft will be extraterrestrial artifacts in other stellar systems," said Dr. Sofia Sheikh, lead author on the paper. "Given that, it is important that we understand the natural distribution of interstellar objects so that we will be able to identify any anomalies that could one day be signs of an artificial interstellar object."

To investigate that possibility, the team observed 3I/ATLAS for more than seven hours using the ATA. The observations covered frequencies from 1 to 9 gigahertz, a range suitable for detecting narrowband radio transmissions. Such signals are not known to occur naturally and would be considered evidence of technology.

Sorting Through Millions of Signals

During the search, researchers detected nearly 74 million narrowband signals. Most were quickly identified as human-made interference.

After removing signals linked to Earth-based technology and narrowing the search to signals that matched the object's motion, only about 200 candidates remained. Further analysis showed that every one of those signals originated from technology on Earth or from satellites orbiting our planet.

No Technosignatures Detected

The search uncovered no evidence of extraterrestrial technology. However, the results provide additional support for the conclusion that 3I/ATLAS is a natural object.

The observations also established new limits on the strength of any possible radio transmitter located on or near 3I/ATLAS. Across the frequencies examined, the study ruled out transmissions stronger than roughly 10-110 watts, comparable to the power consumption of a typical household appliance.

"The results from 3I/ATLAS show how realistic it is to detect a signal with the technology we have today," said Valeria Garcia Lopez, co-author. "That is why it is important to keep searching for technosignatures, even from objects we might not expect to have signals."

Rapid Response to New Interstellar Discoveries

The study highlights the Allen Telescope Array's ability to respond quickly when unusual objects are discovered. Observations of 3I/ATLAS began less than 24 hours after the announcement of its discovery, demonstrating how rapidly the system can be deployed.

Beyond the search for intelligent life, observations of interstellar visitors help scientists better understand the physical characteristics of these objects as they travel through the Solar System.

As astronomers continue finding new interstellar objects, each one provides another opportunity to search for technosignatures while expanding our knowledge of both natural and potentially technological phenomena beyond our Solar System.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific effort to detect extraterrestrial technology using advanced radio telescopes on a rare interstellar object, contributing to our understanding of interstellar visitors and the search for intelligent life. Although no alien signals were found, the rapid-response observations and methodology represent meaningful progress in space science with broad public interest and potential future impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists scan rare interstellar visitor for alien signals, find none but gain new insights
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In July 2025, astronomers discovered 3I/ATLAS, the third known object from another star passing through our Solar System. Scientists are interested in such visitors because they carry clues about other planetary systems and might even hold signs of alien technology.

**What happened** – The SETI Institute used the Allen Telescope Array in California to search 3I/ATLAS for radio signals that might come from intelligent life. Over seven hours, they scanned a wide range of radio frequencies but found no signals beyond human-made interference. They carefully checked millions of signals but confirmed all were from Earth or our satellites.

**Impact** – Although no alien technology was found, the study confirmed 3I/ATLAS is a natural comet-like object. The research also showed how quickly scientists can respond to new interstellar discoveries, starting observations less than a day after the object’s discovery. This fast action improves chances to study future visitors for signs of life or technology.

**What's next step** – As more interstellar objects are found, researchers will continue using similar methods to search for technosignatures—signals from advanced technology. These efforts will also help scientists learn more about the makeup and behavior of objects traveling between stars.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A fast and thorough search of the rare interstellar object 3I/ATLAS found no alien signals but demonstrated new ways to study visitors from other stars.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques scrutent un visiteur interstellaire rare à la recherche de signaux extraterrestres, sans succès mais avec de nouvelles découvertes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En juillet 2025, les astronomes ont découvert 3I/ATLAS, le troisième objet connu venant d’une autre étoile traversant notre Système solaire. Ces visiteurs intéressent les scientifiques car ils portent des indices sur d’autres systèmes planétaires et pourraient même contenir des signes de technologie extraterrestre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Institut SETI a utilisé le réseau de télescopes Allen en Californie pour rechercher des signaux radio provenant de 3I/ATLAS, pouvant indiquer une vie intelligente. Pendant plus de sept heures, ils ont exploré une large gamme de fréquences radio mais n’ont trouvé aucun signal autre que des interférences d’origine humaine. Ils ont examiné des millions de signaux et confirmé que tous venaient de la Terre ou de ses satellites.

**Impact** – Bien qu’aucune technologie extraterrestre n’ait été détectée, l’étude a confirmé que 3I/ATLAS est un objet naturel ressemblant à une comète. La recherche a aussi montré la rapidité avec laquelle les scientifiques peuvent réagir à la découverte d’objets interstellaires, en commençant les observations moins de 24 heures après la découverte. Cette rapidité améliore les chances d’étudier les futurs visiteurs pour y trouver des signes de vie ou de technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – À mesure que d’autres objets interstellaires seront découverts, les chercheurs continueront à utiliser des méthodes similaires pour chercher des technosignatures, c’est-à-dire des signaux provenant de technologies avancées. Ces efforts aideront aussi à mieux comprendre la composition et le comportement des objets voyageant entre les étoiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une recherche rapide et complète de l’objet interstellaire rare 3I/ATLAS n’a trouvé aucun signal extraterrestre mais a montré de nouvelles façons d’étudier les visiteurs d’autres étoiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos escanean raro visitante interestelar en busca de señales alienígenas, sin éxito pero con nuevos aprendizajes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En julio de 2025, los astrónomos descubrieron 3I/ATLAS, el tercer objeto conocido proveniente de otra estrella que pasa por nuestro Sistema Solar. Los científicos están interesados en estos visitantes porque llevan pistas sobre otros sistemas planetarios y podrían tener señales de tecnología alienígena.

**Qué pasó** – El Instituto SETI usó el Arreglo de Telescopios Allen en California para buscar señales de radio en 3I/ATLAS que pudieran venir de vida inteligente. Durante más de siete horas escanearon un amplio rango de frecuencias, pero no encontraron señales más allá de interferencias hechas por humanos. Revisaron millones de señales y confirmaron que todas provenían de la Tierra o sus satélites.

**Impacto** – Aunque no encontraron tecnología alienígena, el estudio confirmó que 3I/ATLAS es un objeto natural parecido a un cometa. La investigación también mostró lo rápido que los científicos pueden responder a nuevos descubrimientos interestelares, comenzando observaciones menos de 24 horas después de su hallazgo. Esta rapidez mejora las oportunidades de estudiar futuros visitantes en busca de señales de vida o tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que se encuentren más objetos interestelares, los investigadores seguirán usando métodos similares para buscar tecnoseñales, señales de tecnología avanzada. Estos esfuerzos también ayudarán a entender mejor la composición y comportamiento de objetos que viajan entre estrellas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una búsqueda rápida y detallada del raro objeto interestelar 3I/ATLAS no encontró señales alienígenas, pero mostró nuevas formas de estudiar visitantes de otras estrellas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a comet-like interstellar object glowing softly as it passes through a stylized Solar System, observed by a cluster of abstract radio telescope dishes arranged in a semicircle beneath a starry sky, with subtle waves of radio signals gently emanating from the telescopes toward the comet, all rendered in natural, muted earth and sky colors.

articles.title: Huge crater in Australia may be the oldest impact structure on Earth
articles.summary: A study claims that the North Pole Dome crater in Western Australia was caused by an asteroid strike 3 billion years ago, but other researchers dispute the proposed age
articles.content:
A study claims that the North Pole Dome crater in Western Australia was caused by an asteroid strike 3 billion years ago, but other researchers dispute the proposed age

The landscape in the North Pole Dome crater in Western Australia Curtin University

A huge crater in Western Australia was created by an asteroid strike 3 billion years ago, according to a mineral-dating technique. This would make it the oldest impact crater on Earth – but other researchers have questioned its proposed age.

The North Pole Dome crater, also known as the Miralga impact structure, was first described by Chris Kirkland at Curtin University in Perth, Australia, and his colleagues in 2025. They estimated that it could be up to 100 kilometres wide.

Advertisement

Kirkland and his team found a layer of rock containing cone-shaped features called shatter cones, which form only after a high-impact event, such as an asteroid strike. Their original study didn’t directly date this rock, but based on correlations with dated rocks in the layers above and below, they proposed that the impact was 3.47 billion years old.

This would make it more than 1.2 billion years older than the Yarrabubba crater in the south of the state, which is regarded as the oldest reliably dated asteroid-strike crater on Earth.

It would also make it the only known impact from the Archaean aeon, a time when the entire planet was a giant but inhospitable water world.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

However, another team, including Aaron Cavosie, also at Curtin University, strongly contested the 3.47-billion-year date. Based on their own analysis of rocks in the area, the team members argue that the impact took place no earlier than 2.77 billion years ago.

Now, Kirkland and his colleagues say they have successfully dated recrystallised minerals at the crater site, which contain shatter cones. “We have now actually looked inside the rocks and tried to find minerals that directly responded to impact in the rock itself, rather than making correlations,” says Kirkland.

Using the rate of the decay of uranium into lead, the team dated zircons within the shatter cones, which recrystallised as a result of the force of the asteroid strike.

They also dated the mineral apatite, which would have formed in the hydrothermal system created by the heat of the impact.

Both the apatite and the zircons returned dates of around 3.02 billion years old, says Kirkland. “So now we’ve got evidence for very hot water percolating through the rocks 3 billion years ago and also evidence for this really unusual heating and recrystallisation process,” he says.

Rocks in the North Pole Dome crater Curtin University

Kirkland says no other known process, such as mountain building or regional metamorphism, easily explains the mineral changes inside the shocked rocks, because there is no evidence that the area was heated or deformed by those processes at about 3.02 billion years ago.

“The only process really that we can link to these mineralogical changes is an impact,” he says. “So that means the best evidence now is a 3-billion-year-old impact, and that by far is the oldest impact crater on the planet.”

Cavosie welcomes the fact that the age of the crater has been revised significantly, but he thinks Kirkland’s team is still overestimating the crater’s age.

“While I’m relieved these authors have backed off their 2025 ‘3.5-billion-year impact’ hypothesis, I don’t think they’ve presented a compelling case for a [3.02-billion-year] impact either,” says Cavosie. “The slow march of science towards the truth thus continues.”

Cavosie says there are clearly shatter cones in younger rocks that are only 2.77 billion years old, which means the impact must have happened after this date.

Alec Brenner at Yale University, who was also part of the group that critiqued the original study, agrees with Cavosie that the rocks must be younger than 2.77 billion years.

“While the new study dismisses this observation because these rocks ‘have not been dated’, they are straightforwardly correlated to nearby rocks that have been dated,” says Brenner.

Kirkland says the key difference is that his team has now directly dated minerals inside the shocked rocks. “The younger age argument still depends on long-distance correlation of undated rocks, largely from satellite-based mapping rather than direct geochemistry or geochronology,” he says. “We now have two mineral clocks from the impact rocks themselves giving the same age. That is why direct dating matters.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the Earth's oldest known asteroid impact crater, providing new direct dating evidence that advances understanding of planetary history. This breakthrough has broad significance for geology, planetary science, and understanding Earth's early environment, making it inspiring and informative for the general public interested in space and Earth's history.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find new evidence for Earth's oldest asteroid crater in Australia
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study impact craters to learn about Earth's history and early environment. The oldest known asteroid crater helps us understand how Earth was shaped billions of years ago.

**What happened** – Researchers at Curtin University in Australia studied the North Pole Dome crater, also called the Miralga impact structure. They used a method that measures the decay of uranium into lead in minerals called zircons and apatite found inside rocks shaped by the asteroid impact. Their new measurements show the crater formed about 3 billion years ago, making it possibly the oldest known asteroid crater on Earth.

**Impact** – This discovery pushes back the age of Earth’s oldest impact crater by hundreds of millions of years. It shows that a giant asteroid hit Earth during the Archaean aeon, a time when the planet was mostly covered by oceans and very different from today. The new dating method directly examined minerals changed by the impact, giving stronger proof than before. This helps scientists better understand how early Earth’s surface and environment were affected by such events.

**What's next step** – Other scientists are still debating the exact age, with some suggesting the crater might be younger. Future research will focus on studying more rocks from the site and nearby areas to confirm the age. This will clarify Earth’s early impact history and improve knowledge about how asteroid strikes influenced the planet’s development.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New direct dating of minerals shows Australia’s North Pole Dome crater formed around 3 billion years ago, possibly making it Earth’s oldest known asteroid impact site.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques trouvent de nouvelles preuves du plus vieux cratère d'astéroïde sur Terre en Australie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les cratères d'impact pour mieux comprendre l'histoire et l'environnement primitif de la Terre. Le plus vieux cratère d'astéroïde connu aide à comprendre comment la Terre s'est formée il y a des milliards d'années.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université Curtin en Australie ont étudié le cratère North Pole Dome, aussi appelé structure d'impact Miralga. Ils ont utilisé une méthode qui mesure la désintégration de l'uranium en plomb dans des minéraux appelés zircons et apatite, présents dans les roches modifiées par l'impact. Leur nouvelle datation montre que le cratère s'est formé il y a environ 3 milliards d'années, ce qui pourrait en faire le plus vieux cratère d'astéroïde connu sur Terre.

**Impact** – Cette découverte repousse l'âge du plus vieux cratère d'impact terrestre de plusieurs centaines de millions d'années. Elle montre qu'un énorme astéroïde a frappé la Terre durant l'éon Archéen, une époque où la planète était principalement recouverte d'océans et très différente d'aujourd'hui. La méthode de datation directe des minéraux modifiés par l'impact apporte une preuve plus solide qu'avant. Cela aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment la surface et l'environnement de la Terre primitive ont été affectés par ces événements.

**Prochaine étape** – Certains scientifiques contestent encore l'âge exact, suggérant que le cratère pourrait être plus jeune. Les recherches futures porteront sur l'étude de plus de roches du site et des environs pour confirmer la datation. Cela clarifiera l'histoire des impacts anciens sur Terre et améliorera la connaissance de l'influence des astéroïdes sur le développement de la planète.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle datation directe des minéraux montre que le cratère North Pole Dome en Australie s'est formé il y a environ 3 milliards d'années, ce qui pourrait en faire le plus vieux cratère d'astéroïde connu sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran nuevas pruebas del cráter de asteroide más antiguo de la Tierra en Australia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian cráteres de impacto para aprender sobre la historia y el ambiente temprano de la Tierra. El cráter de asteroide más antiguo conocido ayuda a entender cómo se formó nuestro planeta hace miles de millones de años.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Curtin en Australia estudiaron el cráter North Pole Dome, también llamado estructura de impacto Miralga. Usaron un método que mide la descomposición de uranio en plomo en minerales llamados circones y apatita, que están dentro de las rocas afectadas por el impacto del asteroide. Sus nuevas mediciones muestran que el cráter se formó hace aproximadamente 3 mil millones de años, posiblemente siendo el cráter de asteroide más antiguo conocido en la Tierra.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento adelanta la edad del cráter de impacto más antiguo de la Tierra por cientos de millones de años. Muestra que un gran asteroide golpeó la Tierra durante el eón Arcaico, cuando el planeta estaba cubierto principalmente por océanos y era muy diferente a hoy. El nuevo método de datación examinó directamente minerales alterados por el impacto, dando una prueba más fuerte que antes. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo los impactos afectaron la superficie y el ambiente de la Tierra primitiva.

**Próximo paso** – Otros científicos aún debaten la edad exacta, sugiriendo que el cráter podría ser más joven. Las futuras investigaciones estudiarán más rocas del lugar y áreas cercanas para confirmar la edad. Esto aclarará la historia temprana de impactos en la Tierra y mejorará el conocimiento sobre cómo los impactos de asteroides influyeron en el desarrollo del planeta.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevas dataciones directas de minerales muestran que el cráter North Pole Dome en Australia se formó hace unos 3 mil millones de años, posiblemente siendo el cráter de impacto más antiguo conocido en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vast ancient Australian landscape featuring a massive, circular crater with layered rock formations and distinctive cone-shaped shatter cones embedded within the rocky walls, illuminated by warm, gentle sunlight filtering through a clear sky, evoking the deep geological history and the powerful asteroid impact from billions of years ago.
